journal-meta.journal-id: Am J Respir Crit Care Med
Am J Respir Crit Care Med

journal-title-group.journal-title: American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine

title-group.article-title: Using the 2024 National Academies of Science, Engineering, and Medicine Definition of Long COVID: Implications for Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine

contrib-group.aff: 1Breathe Chicago Center, Division of Pulmonary, Critical Care, Sleep, and Allergy, and
2Office of Population Health Sciences, University of Illinois Chicago, Chicago, Illinois;
3Department of Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine, China-Japan Friendship Hospital, National Center for Respiratory Medicine, Beijing, China;
4Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore;
5Department of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, Tan Tock Seng Hospital, Singapore;
6Critical Illness, Brain Dysfunction, and Survivorship Center, Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Nashville, Tennessee;
7Veteran Affairs Tennessee Valley Geriatric Research Education Clinical Center, Nashville, Tennessee;
8Faculty of Medicine, National Heart & Lung Institute, St. Mary’s Campus, London, United Kingdom;

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence and requests for reprints should be addressed to Jerry A. Krishnan, M.D., Ph.D., Breathe Chicago Center, University of Illinois Chicago, Mile Square Health Center, Third Floor, 1220 South Wood Street, Chicago, IL 60514. E-mail: jakris@uic.edu.

license.license-p: This article is open access and distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives License 4.0. For commercial usage and reprints, please e-mail Diane Gern (dgern@thoracic.org).

body.p: SARS-CoV-2 emerged in late 2019, leading to the COVID-19 pandemic (1). In the initial months of the pandemic, the number of hospitalizations for respiratory failure caused by acute respiratory distress syndrome increased exponentially, and the global case fatality rate reached 3.4% with large regional variations (2, 3). An unprecedented number of research studies were launched around the globe, aiming to understand and prevent severe acute illness (4–7). Over the past 5 years, the public health impact of acute COVID-19 has evolved considerably, with increases in population-level immunity due to natural infections and the increasing availability of proven effective vaccines, antiviral agents, immunomodulators, monoclonal antibodies, and the ever-changing landscape of SARS-CoV-2 variants with reduced pathogenicity but enhanced immune evasion and infectivity (8–12).

The World Health Organization declared an end of COVID-19 as a global health emergency in May 2023 (13). However, many patients report symptoms lasting months or years after infection with SARS-CoV-2, leading to coining of the term “long COVID” to describe the long-lasting effects on health (14). The cumulative incidence of long COVID is estimated to be about 400 million people worldwide (15). A meta-analysis of 144 studies published from 2021 to 2024 suggested an overall global prevalence of long COVID of 36% among those with a confirmed diagnosis of COVID-19 (16). However, the authors of this report indicated numerous sources of heterogeneity in the prevalence of long COVID across studies, including the definition of “long COVID.” The postacute sequelae are highly variable, including the timing of onset or duration of symptoms, involvement of single to multiple organ systems, and mild to disabling levels of impairments (17). Our understanding of the risk, trajectory, and pathogenesis of long COVID, unfortunately, has been limited and inconsistent across studies.

Various groups have proposed their own terminology and definitions to describe the longer-term adverse health effects of SARS-CoV-2 infection, including “post–COVID-19 syndrome” (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, United Kingdom) (18), “post-COVID condition” (World Health Organization) (19), and postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection or “PASC” (NIH Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery [RECOVER] Initiative, United States) (20). A score-based definition of postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection was developed by the NIH RECOVER team in 2023 (20) and updated in 2024 (21), with the current definition relying on the “RECOVER-Adult Long COVID Research Index” based on 11 symptoms at 4.5 or more months after a first SARS-CoV-2 infection. The RECOVER team also developed different symptom indexes for children aged 6 to 11 years (10 symptoms) and for adolescents (8 symptoms) (22).

More than 200 symptoms have been reported in association with long COVID (23), raising concerns that definitions proposed by the RECOVER team to improve specificity on the basis of a selective list of symptoms will unwittingly exclude some individuals with significant chronic health impairments after SARS-CoV-2 infection. In the absence of validated biomarkers (a limitation of the current state of science), use of selective symptom lists would also limit the ability of individuals with chronic sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 to successfully apply for disability benefits or contribute to further research for patients with long COVID. In addition, some groups have proposed long COVID as a diagnosis of exclusion (18, 19), which does not consider the biologic plausibility that infection with SARS-CoV-2 may exacerbate preexisting illnesses. The minimum duration of symptoms to diagnose long COVID is also highly variable across definitions (e.g., 3 mo [18], 2 mo [19], no specific duration [21]). The lack of consensus about the terminology and definition has been a barrier to clinical care and has slowed research in long COVID.

In response to these various concerns, the U.S. federal government sponsored the National Academies of Science, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM), a private, U.S.-based nonprofit organization, to develop a consensus-based definition, with input from patients and an interdisciplinary group of experts. After 18 months of deliberation involving over 1,300 stakeholders and an extensive literature review (24), NASEM recommended the adoption of the patient-coined term “long COVID” and offered the following symptom-based definition: Long COVID is “an infection-associated chronic condition (IACC) that occurs after SARS-CoV-2 infection and is present for at least 3 months as a continuous, relapsing and remitting, or progressive disease state that affects one or more organ systems.” The 2024 NASEM definition applies to both pediatric and adult populations. A table comparing the 2024 NASEM long COVID definition with those of other groups is available in a previous publication developed by one of the authors of the present report (E.W.E.) (24).

Because there are currently no biomarkers to include or exclude long COVID (25), the NASEM committee prioritized the importance of ensuring that all patients who experience long COVID will be included in the definition (i.e., high sensitivity), so that they may receive appropriate medical and social supports and be eligible for research. The committee acknowledged the opportunity for false-positive findings (i.e., low specificity) and recommended a revision of the definition within 3 years or sooner if relevant information is available. For example, the availability of biomarkers and their integration into tests that reliably identify a previous infection weeks, months, or years earlier could greatly improve the specificity of a long COVID definition, particularly in populations with inadequate access to or use of tests during their initial infection with SARS-CoV-2. The pathogenesis of long COVID may be highly variable (e.g., specific organ damage, thromboinflammation, SARS-CoV-2 persistence, reactivation of other viruses, alteration of the microbiome, autoimmunity, serotonin deficiency) across individual patients and is inadequately understood; we refer interested readers to other reports (26–33).

As an international team of clinicians and researchers who authored this pulmonary perspective, we advocate for using a standardized definition of long COVID as the basis for patient care and research. We acknowledge that the 2024 NASEM symptom-based definition of long COVID has limitations because of the current state of science. Nevertheless, we view the 2024 NASEM definition as a reasonable starting point to propose frameworks for clinicians and researchers in the pulmonary and critical care community. We hope this report catalyzes global collaborations to fill key knowledge gaps so that more patients around the world can benefit from the latest advancements in care.

sec.title: Framework for Clinical Evaluation
sec.p: In the initial months of the COVID-19 pandemic, there were no tests that could reliably rule in or exclude SARS-CoV-2 infection. Even after highly sensitive and specific RT-PCR tests were developed, their availability was insufficient to meet the need for testing across the world. Rapid antigen tests started to become available in some parts of the world in the second half of 2020, offering the convenience of at-home testing. However, the sensitivity of rapid antigen tests for SARS-CoV-2 is estimated to be only 47% when compared with RT-PCR (34). Therefore, many millions were likely infected across the world with highly pathogenic variants of SARS-CoV-2 (and therefore at increased risk of long COVID) before they could be diagnosed. Accordingly, the 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID does not require objective confirmation of SARS-CoV-2 infection to avoid the exacerbating health inequities in historically disadvantaged populations.

Also, long COVID may exacerbate preexisting comorbid diseases, so the 2024 NASEM definition does not consider long COVID as a diagnosis of exclusion. The time course of long COVID appears to be highly variable. Fortunately, most patients with acute symptoms of COVID-19 fully recover after a period of days to weeks after the initial illness without further sequelae (i.e., do not develop long COVID). Those with long COVID may present with ongoing symptoms that continue after the initial illness or symptoms that start weeks or months after an apparent initial recovery. Symptoms of long COVID may be continuous (persistent, progressively worsening, or progressively improving); present with a relapsing (at times worse) and remitting (at other times improve or disappear altogether) pattern over hours, days, weeks, or longer; or start after an apparent resolution of the initial episode of COVID-19. The trajectory also may be symptom specific. For example, cough may improve and resolve over a period of weeks to months, whereas exercise intolerance and fatigue may be persistent. The NASEM committee recommends a minimum of 3 months’ duration of symptoms or a 3-month period of relapsing and remitting symptoms after infection with SARS-CoV-2 before a diagnosis of long COVID can be made. A 3-month cutoff was intended to provide sufficient time for most patients to recover from acute manifestations of SARS-CoV-2 infection and for evaluation and initial management of conditions that may present with symptoms similar to long COVID.

Currently, the principles for evaluating a patient with respiratory symptoms after SARS-CoV-2 infection are no different from any other clinical scenario. The role of the pulmonologist is to obtain a history, perform a clinical examination, and judiciously use investigations to identify pathology to provide the patient with some idea of the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment options. It is important to recognize that the clinical work-up may or may not identify an abnormality associated with presenting respiratory symptoms; also, abnormalities that are detected may not be related to a prior SARS CoV-2 infection. All of this could change once reliable biomarkers for long COVID are identified.

It is acknowledged that in the case of long COVID, many abnormalities that will be identified are unlikely to have treatment options because they are largely sequelae of organ damage, but this may change with future research. If specific nonpulmonary syndromes are identified, such as resting tachycardia or orthostatic intolerance in the absence of dehydration or other causes of hypovolemia, then a referral can be made to an appropriate specialist (e.g., electrophysiologist) to evaluate a patient for postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome using a tilt table test. The clinical challenge is when identifiable pathology is absent or is insufficient to explain the patient’s symptoms, which is not unique to long COVID. What the NASEM guidelines explicitly make clear is that the absence of identifiable pathology does not rule out the diagnosis of long COVID, because the diagnosis of long COVID is based on a patient’s symptoms and temporal relationship to an episode of SARS-CoV-2 infection.

Pulmonologists are likely to encounter a patient with long COVID with unexplained fatigue, breathlessness, chronic cough, or exercise intolerance. In a minority of cases, such symptoms are related to postinfectious interstitial lung disease (ILD)– and acute respiratory distress syndrome–related pulmonary fibrosis or the physical and neuropsychological impairments similar to those seen in the post–intensive care syndrome. Whether such presentations are different after SARS-CoV-2 infection compared with other etiologies (e.g., severe community-acquired bacterial pneumonia) is unclear. Other long COVID symptom clusters (e.g., neurocognitive impairment, syncope) will generally present to other specialists as appropriate. Our recommendations for clinical evaluation of long COVID in the present report are the opinion of the authors and based on a reasonable assessment of patient with the symptoms as described above. Although there are publications of a variety of highly specialized testing in patients with long COVID (such as magnetic resonance angiography, cardiopulmonary exercise testing, hyperpolarized gas imaging), these should be considered as research tools because their utility in long COVID has not been established. As with all conditions, clinicians are encouraged to engage patients in shared decision making when considering various options (Figure 1). The authors acknowledge the need for studies to assess the utility of the proposed approach to evaluation, including in low-resource settings, and revisions to the clinical framework as validated biomarkers of long COVID emerge. We also encourage clinicians to consider referral to a long COVID support group, a multidisciplinary long COVID clinic, and, when eligible, to join a research study when available. We refer readers to other reports that have described potential approaches to managing long COVID–associated conditions, including respiratory symptoms (35), dysautonomia (36), cardiovascular complications (37), and neurocognitive symptoms (38, 39). Results of a systematic review of clinical trials suggest the potential for cognitive behavioral therapy and rehabilitation to improve physical and mental health in adults with long COVID; however, the authors of the review were concerned about the risk of bias because of a lack of masking or adequacy of attention controls as well as the applicability of findings to various subgroups of populations with long COVID (e.g., predominant symptom clusters or level of disability) (40). Evidence-based recommendations for management of long COVID will therefore require the results of rigorously performed clinical trials with adequate attention to internal validity (to minimize risk of bias) and external validity (generalizability).

caption.p: Approach to clinical evaluation. The approach to evaluation described in the figure is based on expert opinion. The sequence and comprehensiveness of the evaluation should be tailored to the individual patient using a shared decision-making process based on resources available in the clinical setting and patient preferences and values. The approach should be updated as evidence from rigorously performed studies becomes available to guide practice (e.g., validated biomarkers for diagnosis or response to therapy, clinical trials).

sec.title: Framework for Research, Including Clinical Trials
sec.p:
Currently, the unknowns are far greater than the knowns regarding long COVID. The 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID serves as scaffold on which we can build studies to better understand the burden and biologic pathways that contribute to SARS-CoV-2–associated chronic conditions and the harms and benefits of potential interventions. Key considerations for design of research in long COVID are outlined below.
Use the 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID: The definition is intentionally broad. Researchers, however, are advised to use study inclusion and exclusion criteria to select the appropriate segment of the long COVID population that is fit for purpose. For example, for studies to understand SARS-CoV-2– associated postviral ILD, inclusion criteria should include results of imaging studies (including high-resolution chest computed tomography) that confirm findings consistent with ILD.
Report whether the diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 was “suspected,” “probable,” or “confirmed” (World Health Organization categorization) (41): Many patients are now not being formally tested, with significant implications for the rising risk of misclassifying exposure (SARS-CoV-2 infection). However, for studies to understand the extent to which viral persistence contributes to the pathogenesis of long COVID or clinical trials of antiviral interventions, researchers may wish to limit participation to individuals with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection (e.g., positive RT-PCR or positive SARS-CoV-2 antigen test result during acute illness).
Describe the period (dates) of acute COVID-19 and other factors that contribute to secular trends in the severity of initial illness: Studies should specify dates of enrollment and follow-up, locations at which participants were enrolled, and, ideally, the predominant viral variants at each field site and treatments, if any, for SARS-CoV-2 and for supportive care (e.g., use of treatment-dose anticoagulation in hospitalized patients).
Characterize preexisting health conditions and risk factors for COVID-19: Standardizing the collection and reporting of these data would help with interpreting study results and support cross-study synthesis of findings. For example, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention maintains a list of underlying conditions that predispose individuals to severe COVID-19 (42).
Select an appropriate comparator group when making claims about COVID-19– and long COVID–specific findings: Depending on the research question, consider matching long COVID and comparator groups on the basis of dates of selection into the study to account for factors that contribute to secular changes in disease severity. These factors include vaccination status against SARS-CoV-2 before the index SARS-CoV-2 infection, severity of acute illness (hospitalized or outpatient), treatments received, duration of symptoms, and preexisting conditions.
Characterize long COVID: Characterize long COVID on the basis of symptoms/symptom clusters, duration of symptoms, severity, trajectory, and any supportive treatment. We also recommend that investigators describe the study population that is enrolled using the symptom-based NIH RECOVER index (21, 22), which may facilitate subsequent cross-study syntheses of results. Outcomes with known measurement properties in long COVID or relevant conditions should be employed (if unavailable in long COVID). For example, to assess neurocognitive impairment, investigators could use the CogState brief battery, which has been validated in patients with mild traumatic brain injury and AIDS dementia complex (43). To assess functional exercise capacity and tolerance, investigators could use the 6-minute-walk test, which is widely used to measure response to medical interventions in patients with moderate or severe heart or lung disease (44).
Report results of pulmonary function tests and imaging: Although long COVID is defined on the basis of symptoms, we recommend, when possible, collecting and reporting results of pre- and post-bronchodilator spirometry, lung volumes, DlCO, and high-resolution chest computed tomography. Such information will help to relate the patient’s symptoms with well-accepted measures of lung disease.

Numerous studies are underway to fill the evidence gaps, including over 60 studies about the pathogenesis of long COVID (45, 46) and 8 clinical trials of 13 interventions by the NIH RECOVER consortium (47). More such studies are needed, including those conducted in other countries and by multinational consortia, because the presentation of long COVID and context of health care may vary by geographic region. We recommend establishing additional research infrastructures, including international consortia, to promote knowledge sharing as a key next step. The consortia may include various sections that may focus on 1) epidemiology, 2) pathogenesis, 3) clinical trials, 4) clinical guideline development, and 5) dissemination and implementation science. Because some features of long COVID are shared with other disease states (e.g., myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome, post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome, HIV/AIDS), collaborations with other research groups or experts in these areas may help to unlock synergies. Early and continuous patient engagement as research partners is critical to ensuring that the studies are designed to address the health concerns of those with the lived experience and are feasible for people with long COVID to join. For example, long study visits (e.g., 4 h or more) may be impractical in patients with postexertional malaise or other disabling conditions. Given the heterogeneous nature of long COVID driven by different pathophysiological mechanisms (i.e., multiple subtypes) and degrees of impairment, clinical trials using innovative designs, such as the use of master protocols and adaptive trial platforms to efficiently study multiple therapies, multiple diseases, or both, should be considered (48).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In the past 5 years, significant progress has been made in preventing and treating acute COVID-19, the illness caused by SARS-CoV-2. However, advancements in the evaluation and management of persistent respiratory symptoms and other chronic conditions known as long COVID have been comparatively slower. The recently introduced 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID provides a basis for a crucial framework for clinical and research settings. Moving forward, research must adopt a multidisciplinary approach, prioritizing patient perspectives and fostering international collaborations. Innovative clinical trial designs that employ master protocols and leverage global consortia will be vital in translating research into tangible improvements in care and support for individuals affected by long COVID.

sec.title: Acknowledgment
sec.p: Drs. Krishnan and Ely served on the committee that developed the 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID.

fn.p: Artificial Intelligence Disclaimer: No artificial intelligence tools were used in writing this manuscript.
Originally Published in Press as DOI: 10.1164/rccm.202410-2036PP on May 29, 2025
Author disclosures are available with the text of this article at www.atsjournals.org.
